author: @bsc4344
text:
i don’t know why but the last few posts of yours reminds me of an uber simple LED light source some one created thru a kind of UN program, to distribute free around primitive parts of Africa so the hut dwellers didn’t have to burn sooty dung for illumination any more (and get lung damage understandably)
Saw it in a show ages ago.
Was a power feed  to the simple LED(s), energy from (a basket i think?) that is loaded with rocks when “at the top” like those old weight driven clocks, and maybe a flywheel is spun as the weight super slowly lowers to the ground? Pretty much it, very few parts, then again memory over a decade or two isn’t infallible. 
Had a crazy long powering time. Seems to me that would be neat for a cheap light source in my own home. Your recent projects make me think this can be pulled out of my memory and tinkered with. Thanks for your inspirations.

author: @paulsharpe3794
text: Hi there I used to have a small clock with this pendulum. The top bars were slightly rotated to cach the string to cause it to  rape anty clockwise looking from the top. It would then unwind and cach the bottom rod and wrap clockwise. So the string wraps around the 2 bottom rods for a total of 4 times for one rotation of the pendulum arm. This will increase the amount of time it takes to unwind the clock. I hope this will help you to understand what the top 2 rods are doing. It fascinated me .unfortunately it was lost in a house move.

author: @ΜικεΡ
text:
I remember building a similar mechanism out of Lego. Iirc it used a wind-up box instead of gravity. I dont think it used a top frame either, but then again I think it was more about building the mechanism than keeping time. Maybe the top bars make the winding more consistent? 

On a tangentially related note, my new favorite way to keep time are radio signals. Theres a few atomic clocks broadcasting around the world, and the circuitry to pick that up ought to be straightforward. (Unfortunately i havent gotten around to actually building a project, just some research)
GPS is another fantastic source for time, because those satellites are essentially just atomic clocks in orbit.
Im hoping I can pick up WWVB indoors because thats the greatest weakness of a GPS clock, you basically need line-of-sight to the sky.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @totherarf
text:
One of the great joys I find in life is being challenged to "think about stuff". Especially stuff I would not otherwise do! Thanks Rob, for that!

It seems to me that we do not actually need an escapement but just a regulator. 
The device you made has a crucial difference from "normal" clockwork mechanisms in that the motion is all in one direction rather than reciprocal in nature.
I seem to remember looking at some clocks that used air resistance as a regulator. They did this by having several tiny blades at right angles to the motion so that the faster they spun the more resistance to the spin there was. I suspect that this was even more fiddly to trim to any good degree of accuracy ...... but there are other mechanisms that might be better!

As a fan of Steampunk I love those Victorian machines that used a spinning weight cantilever to regulate speed and wonder if that approach might be handy here ..... especially if made from shiny brass! ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
